But the Mormon militia DID disguise themselves as native Americans and slaughter 200 Arkansas pioneers in a meadow in the town now known as Central, UT. That is a fact. The Mormons were being prosecuted by the US government and all kinds of paranoid, leading to their actions. Did Brigham young himself give the order? We’ll likely never know.

video_title: American Primeval | Official Mature-Rated Trailer | Netflix
reply_text: @MarWode my home is in the town of Central, UT — 3 miles down the road from the mountain meadow massacre. I’ve been there and I’ve done research out of pure curiosity. The show dramatizes and changes the actual events, that’s correct.  There was actually a long standoff between the Mormon militia and Arkansas pioneers. The pioneers created a circle barrier with their wagons and held out for days. The militia finally convinced the Arkansas pioneers to surrender when they ran low on food and water, promising them safe passage if they lay down their weapons. Then they separated the men from the women and children, and slaughtered everyone over the age of 6. There are 2 separate burial sites. One for the men, and one for the women further up the meadow. The church tried to tell a different story for a very long time, but surviving kin of those Arkansas pioneers finally got the plaques and history books corrected.

reply_text: ​@GobbostopperThere's a lot to the history, and it wasn't one-sided but the original commenter's remark about the latter-day saints (referred to as "Mormons" above) being "brutal and ruthless" is not representative.  The latter-day saints had been treated violently before being driven out of their previous homes in the eastern U.S.  They didn't want more violence when they traveled west, but conflict is apparently natural, particularly when different cultures interact, so while they had plenty of positive interaction, such as trading, there were also problems.  I have a journal from an ancestor (Anson Call) that describes interactions with native peoples and some violence.  He records discouraging violence among different parties.  In one instance, he recorded being "annoyed a great deal by the passing of Emigrants to California" and that they killed several native men and stole from them despite him telling them "not to molest them", and then later the sons of the men who were killed went out for vengeance and killed a group of soldiers including Captain Gunnison.  When my ancestor, who found and cleaned up the remains of the soldiers, explained to the natives who they had killed, they "manifested much sorrow, and said they were satisfied".  My ancestor encouraged peace even when his brother Josiah was killed quite violently by natives, after which he recorded, "This is the first instance of the kind that has happened to any citizens of this place since its settlement."  The latter-day saints went west to get away from violence.  There were some bad events, but they mostly wanted to live in peace like anyone else.  This series is fictionalized and evidently wants to depict violence specifically.
